title: NavMesh & AI: Pathfinding, Agents & Enemy Behavior
html:
Why pathfinding matters

Up to now your enemies have moved in straight lines or along scripted paths. A third-person shooter needs smarter foes: they should weave around crates, take cover, lose sight of you behind walls, and only attack when they can actually see you. Hand-coding obstacle avoidance is a nightmare. Unity solves this with the NavMesh (navigation mesh) — a baked, walkable surface the engine builds from your level geometry — plus the NavMeshAgent component that steers along it using A* pathfinding.

Baking a NavMesh

The classic workflow lives in Window > AI > Navigation. Mark static level geometry as Navigation Static, then in the Bake tab set agent radius, height, max slope, and step height, and click Bake. Unity carves a blue mesh over every walkable surface, automatically cutting holes around obstacles. The modern approach uses the AI Navigation package: add a NavMeshSurface component to a parent object and bake at runtime or in-editor — this supports multiple agent sizes and runtime regeneration, which you need for procedural levels.

Tip.
Re-bake whenever you move static geometry. For doors or destructible cover, use a NavMeshObstacle with Carve enabled so the agent reroutes in real time without a full re-bake.

The NavMeshAgent

Drop a NavMeshAgent on your enemy. Set its destination and the agent handles steering, acceleration, and avoidance for you.

EnemyChase.cs
// Minimal chase: follow the player every frame using UnityEngine; using UnityEngine.AI; [RequireComponent(typeof(NavMeshAgent))] public class EnemyChase : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] Transform target; NavMeshAgent agent; void Awake() => agent = GetComponent<NavMeshAgent>(); void Update() { if (target != null) agent.SetDestination(target.position); } }

Perception: only chase what you can see

A real enemy should not have x-ray vision. We give it a field of view check: the target must be within range, inside a view cone, and not blocked by walls (a Physics.Raycast against an obstacle layer).

Perception.cs
using UnityEngine; public class Perception : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] Transform target; [SerializeField] float viewRadius = 15f; [SerializeField] float viewAngle = 90f; [SerializeField] LayerMask obstacleMask; public bool CanSeeTarget() { if (target == null) return false; Vector3 dir = target.position - transform.position; if (dir.magnitude > viewRadius) return false; if (Vector3.Angle(transform.forward, dir) > viewAngle * 0.5f) return false; // Line of sight blocked by a wall? if (Physics.Raycast(transform.position + Vector3.up, dir.normalized, dir.magnitude, obstacleMask)) return false; return true; } }

A finite state machine

Enemies feel alive when they transition between behaviors. The cleanest pattern for this scale is a finite state machine (FSM): an enum of states plus a switch that decides what to do and when to change. Patrol until you see the player, chase until you are in attack range, attack while in range, and return to patrol when you lose sight.

EnemyAI.cs
using UnityEngine; using UnityEngine.AI; [RequireComponent(typeof(NavMeshAgent), typeof(Perception))] public class EnemyAI : MonoBehaviour { enum State { Patrol, Chase, Attack } State state = State.Patrol; [SerializeField] Transform[] waypoints; [SerializeField] Transform player; [SerializeField] float attackRange = 2.5f; [SerializeField] float attackCooldown = 1.2f; NavMeshAgent agent; Perception eyes; int wpIndex; float nextAttack; void Awake() { agent = GetComponent<NavMeshAgent>(); eyes = GetComponent<Perception>(); } void Update() { bool sees = eyes.CanSeeTarget(); float dist = Vector3.Distance(transform.position, player.position); switch (state) { case State.Patrol: Patrol(); if (sees) state = State.Chase; break; case State.Chase: agent.SetDestination(player.position); if (dist <= attackRange) state = State.Attack; else if (!sees) state = State.Patrol; break; case State.Attack: agent.SetDestination(transform.position); // stop transform.LookAt(player); if (Time.time >= nextAttack) { Fire(); nextAttack = Time.time + attackCooldown; } if (dist > attackRange) state = State.Chase; break; } } void Patrol() { if (waypoints.Length == 0) return; if (!agent.pathPending && agent.remainingDistance < 0.5f) { wpIndex = (wpIndex + 1) % waypoints.Length; agent.SetDestination(waypoints[wpIndex].position); } } void Fire() => Debug.Log("Bang! at " + player.name); }
Lab.
Add a fourth state, Search: when the enemy loses sight mid-chase, have it walk to the player's last known position (cache it the frame sees goes false) and look around for two seconds before falling back to Patrol. This single change makes AI feel dramatically smarter.
Tip.
Use agent.isStopped and agent.velocity.magnitude to drive your Animator's locomotion blend tree (from lesson 2.9) so the walk/run animation matches actual movement speed.

Off-mesh links and avoidance

Gaps a NavMesh cannot cross — jumping over a fence, dropping off a ledge — are bridged with Off-Mesh Links. When agents bunch up, tune Avoidance Priority (lower numbers win) and Obstacle Avoidance Quality so a squad does not jostle into one clump.

🎮 Build This: Third-person shooter (3D) — Hunter's Hollow

This starts a new genre: the third-person shooter (3D). You will build a behind-the-shoulder player using a Cinemachine FreeLook camera (from lesson 2.10) and populate an arena with NavMesh-driven guards.

  1. Block out a small arena with crates and walls; mark everything static and bake a NavMeshSurface.
  2. Make a third-person player: a capsule with a FreeLook camera and movement relative to the camera's yaw.
  3. Create an enemy prefab with NavMeshAgent, Perception, and EnemyAI; lay out patrol waypoints.
  4. Wire Fire() to a hitscan raycast (reuse the FPS damage code from lesson 2.16) so guards actually shoot back.
  5. Add the Search state from the Lab and confirm guards investigate your last position when you break line of sight.
  6. Drive the enemy Animator from agent.velocity for idle/walk/shoot states.

title: 3D Racing: Wheel Colliders, Vehicle Physics & Track Systems
html:
Cars are not just boxes with velocity

You could fake a car by adding force to a Rigidbody, but it would feel like a hovercraft. Real driving feel comes from suspension, grip, and weight transfer — and Unity gives you all of it through the specialized WheelCollider. This lesson builds a genuinely drivable 3D car with four wheel colliders, then layers on a checkpoint and lap system so it becomes a real racing game.

Anatomy of a car GameObject

The hierarchy is specific. A root Car object holds a single Rigidbody (the whole car's mass, typically 1000-1500 kg) and a low center of mass so it does not flip. Under it sit four empty WheelCollider objects positioned at each wheel, plus four separate visual wheel meshes that we rotate and reposition in code to match the colliders. Keeping physics wheels and visual wheels separate is the key trick — the collider does the physics, the mesh just follows it.

Law.
Set the Rigidbody's center of mass low (e.g. rb.centerOfMass = new Vector3(0, -0.5f, 0)). A high center of mass makes the car roll over on the first corner — this is the single most common beginner mistake.

WheelCollider tuning

Each WheelCollider exposes a suspension spring (how stiff the ride is), damper (how quickly it settles), and two friction curves — forwardFriction (acceleration and braking grip) and sidewaysFriction (cornering grip). Raise sideways stiffness for arcade grip; lower it for drifting. We drive the car by setting motorTorque on the rear wheels, steerAngle on the front wheels, and brakeTorque on all four.

CarController.cs
using UnityEngine; public class CarController : MonoBehaviour { [System.Serializable] public class Axle { public WheelCollider left, right; public Transform leftMesh, rightMesh; public bool motor; // does this axle apply drive torque? public bool steering;// does this axle steer? } [SerializeField] Axle[] axles; [SerializeField] float maxMotorTorque = 1500f; [SerializeField] float maxSteerAngle = 30f; [SerializeField] float brakeTorque = 3000f; Rigidbody rb; void Start() { rb = GetComponent<Rigidbody>(); rb.centerOfMass = new Vector3(0, -0.5f, 0); } void FixedUpdate() { float throttle = Input.GetAxis("Vertical"); float steer = Input.GetAxis("Horizontal") * maxSteerAngle; bool braking = Input.GetKey(KeyCode.Space); foreach (var axle in axles) { if (axle.steering) { axle.left.steerAngle = steer; axle.right.steerAngle = steer; } if (axle.motor) { axle.left.motorTorque = throttle * maxMotorTorque; axle.right.motorTorque = throttle * maxMotorTorque; } float b = braking ? brakeTorque : 0f; axle.left.brakeTorque = axle.right.brakeTorque = b; UpdateMesh(axle.left, axle.leftMesh); UpdateMesh(axle.right, axle.rightMesh); } } // Match the visual wheel to the physics wheel's world pose void UpdateMesh(WheelCollider col, Transform mesh) { if (mesh == null) return; col.GetWorldPose(out Vector3 pos, out Quaternion rot); mesh.SetPositionAndRotation(pos, rot); } }
Tip.
All physics — torque, steering, GetWorldPose — belongs in FixedUpdate, not Update. WheelColliders simulate on the physics step; reading or writing them off-step produces jitter.

The lap and checkpoint system

A race needs to know you actually drove the whole track, not cut the corner. The standard solution is a ring of invisible trigger checkpoints you must pass in order; crossing the start/finish only counts a lap if you hit every checkpoint since the last one.

Checkpoint.cs & LapManager.cs
using UnityEngine; public class Checkpoint : MonoBehaviour { public int index; void OnTriggerEnter(Collider other) { if (other.CompareTag("Player")) LapManager.Instance.PassCheckpoint(index); } } public class LapManager : MonoBehaviour { public static LapManager Instance; [SerializeField] int totalCheckpoints = 8; [SerializeField] int totalLaps = 3; int nextIndex = 0; int lap = 0; void Awake() => Instance = this; public void PassCheckpoint(int index) { if (index != nextIndex) return; // out of order: ignore (anti-cheat) nextIndex++; if (nextIndex >= totalCheckpoints) { nextIndex = 0; lap++; Debug.Log($"Lap {lap}/{totalLaps}"); if (lap >= totalLaps) Finish(); } } void Finish() => Debug.Log("Race complete!"); }
Lab.
Add a respawn at last checkpoint feature: store each checkpoint's transform, and when the player presses R (or falls below the track), teleport the car to the last passed checkpoint with zero velocity (rb.velocity = rb.angularVelocity = Vector3.zero) facing the track direction.

Game feel for speed

Tie engine pitch to rb.velocity.magnitude, widen the camera FOV slightly at high speed for a rush, and use a Cinemachine camera that lags behind the car. Add skid-mark trails when WheelHit.sidewaysSlip (from GetGroundHit) exceeds a threshold.

🎮 Build This: Racing (3D) — Apex Circuit

This starts a new genre: the 3D racing/car game, built from scratch with real vehicle physics.

  1. Build a car root with a Rigidbody, four WheelColliders, and four visual wheel meshes; lower the center of mass.
  2. Add CarController, configure front axle as steering and rear axle as motor, and tune suspension and friction until it feels planted.
  3. Lay out a looping track; place 8 box-trigger checkpoints in order plus a start/finish.
  4. Add LapManager and Checkpoint; confirm only in-order laps count.
  5. Implement the respawn Lab so going off-track is recoverable.
  6. Add a chase camera, speed-based engine pitch, and skid trails for game feel.

title: Strategy / Tower Defense: Waves, Targeting & Resource Economy
html:
The three pillars of tower defense

Tower defense is a deceptively rich genre that exercises systems you will reuse everywhere: spawning enemies in timed waves, autonomous tower targeting, and a resource economy that gates player choices. Get these three loops talking to each other and you have a real strategy game. We will build it with clean, decoupled managers so each piece can be tested alone.

Enemies that follow a path

You can use the NavMesh from lesson 2.17, but classic tower defense uses an explicit waypoint path for predictable, designer-controlled routes. An enemy walks from waypoint to waypoint; reaching the end damages the player's base.

Enemy.cs
using UnityEngine; public class Enemy : MonoBehaviour { public static System.Action<Enemy> OnDeath; public static System.Action<int> OnReachEnd; [SerializeField] float speed = 3f; [SerializeField] float maxHealth = 30f; [SerializeField] int reward = 10; [SerializeField] int leakDamage = 1; Transform[] path; int wp = 0; float health; public void Init(Transform[] waypoints) { path = waypoints; health = maxHealth; transform.position = path[0].position; } void Update() { if (path == null || wp >= path.Length) return; Vector3 t = path[wp].position; transform.position = Vector3.MoveTowards(transform.position, t, speed * Time.deltaTime); if (Vector3.Distance(transform.position, t) < 0.05f) { wp++; if (wp >= path.Length) { OnReachEnd?.Invoke(leakDamage); Destroy(gameObject); } } } public int Reward => reward; public void TakeDamage(float dmg) { health -= dmg; if (health <= 0f) { OnDeath?.Invoke(this); Destroy(gameObject); } } }
Tip.
Notice the static Action events. Towers, the economy, and the UI all subscribe to OnDeath/OnReachEnd without the Enemy knowing they exist — this is the event-channel decoupling from lesson 2.11 applied to gameplay.

The wave spawner

Waves are data: a list of how many enemies, of which prefab, and how fast. A coroutine (from lesson 2.12) drives the timing beautifully.

WaveSpawner.cs
using System.Collections; using UnityEngine; public class WaveSpawner : MonoBehaviour { [System.Serializable] public class Wave { public Enemy prefab; public int count = 10; public float interval = 0.7f; } [SerializeField] Wave[] waves; [SerializeField] Transform[] path; [SerializeField] float timeBetweenWaves = 8f; IEnumerator Start() { foreach (var wave in waves) { for (int i = 0; i < wave.count; i++) { Enemy e = Instantiate(wave.prefab); e.Init(path); yield return new WaitForSeconds(wave.interval); } yield return new WaitForSeconds(timeBetweenWaves); } } }

Targeting logic

A tower scans for enemies in range and picks one by a targeting priority — closest, first (furthest along the path), or lowest health. Then it fires on a cooldown.

Tower.cs
using UnityEngine; public class Tower : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] float range = 5f; [SerializeField] float damage = 10f; [SerializeField] float fireRate = 1.5f; // shots per second [SerializeField] LayerMask enemyMask; float cooldown; void Update() { cooldown -= Time.deltaTime; Enemy target = FindTarget(); if (target != null) { transform.LookAt(target.transform); if (cooldown <= 0f) { target.TakeDamage(damage); cooldown = 1f / fireRate; } } } Enemy FindTarget() { Collider[] hits = Physics.OverlapSphere(transform.position, range, enemyMask); Enemy best = null; float bestDist = Mathf.Infinity; foreach (var h in hits) { float d = Vector3.Distance(transform.position, h.transform.position); if (d < bestDist) { bestDist = d; best = h.GetComponent<Enemy>(); } } return best; // 'closest' priority } }

The economy and placement

Gold ties it together: kills pay out, leaks cost lives, and building or upgrading towers costs gold. A single GameEconomy owns the numbers and broadcasts changes to the UI.

GameEconomy.cs
using UnityEngine; public class GameEconomy : MonoBehaviour { public static GameEconomy Instance; public static System.Action<int,int> OnChanged; // gold, lives [SerializeField] int gold = 100; [SerializeField] int lives = 20; void Awake() => Instance = this; void OnEnable() { Enemy.OnDeath += e => { gold += e.Reward; OnChanged?.Invoke(gold, lives); }; Enemy.OnReachEnd += dmg => { lives -= dmg; OnChanged?.Invoke(gold, lives); if (lives <= 0) Debug.Log("Game Over"); }; } public bool TrySpend(int cost) { if (gold < cost) return false; gold -= cost; OnChanged?.Invoke(gold, lives); return true; } }
Lab.
Add tower placement: raycast from the mouse to a ground layer, snap to a grid cell, show a translucent ghost tower, and on click call GameEconomy.Instance.TrySpend(cost) — only instantiate the real tower if it returns true. Refuse placement on occupied cells or on the enemy path.
Watch out.
Always unsubscribe static events when the object is destroyed (OnDisable) or reloading the scene will stack duplicate handlers and gold will multiply per reload.
🎮 Build This: Strategy / Tower Defense — Last Stand

This starts a new genre: strategy / tower defense, a top-down or angled-camera defense game.

  1. Lay an empty-object waypoint path from a spawn point to your base; tag the base.
  2. Make an enemy prefab with Enemy; verify it walks the path and leaks damage at the end.
  3. Add WaveSpawner with two or three escalating waves of different prefabs.
  4. Build a tower prefab with Tower; confirm it acquires and damages the nearest enemy.
  5. Add GameEconomy and a uGUI HUD (lesson 2.13) showing live gold and lives via OnChanged.
  6. Implement the placement Lab so the player spends gold to build towers on a grid.

title: RPG Systems: Stats, Inventory, Dialogue & Turn-Based Combat
html:
An RPG is four systems in a trench coat

Role-playing games are the ultimate integration exercise: character stats, an inventory of items, branching dialogue, and turn-based combat all feeding each other. The secret to keeping it sane is data-driven design with ScriptableObjects (lesson 2.11) — every item, stat block, and NPC line lives as an asset you can author without touching code.

Stats as data

Define a stat block as a ScriptableObject so designers can create Goblin, Hero, and Dragon assets in the Project window. Runtime characters hold a mutable copy plus current HP.

StatBlock.cs & Combatant.cs
using UnityEngine; [CreateAssetMenu(menuName = "RPG/StatBlock")] public class StatBlock : ScriptableObject { public string displayName; public int maxHealth = 30; public int attack = 8; public int defense = 3; public int speed = 5; } public class Combatant : MonoBehaviour { public StatBlock stats; public int currentHP; public bool IsAlive => currentHP > 0; void Awake() => currentHP = stats.maxHealth; public int TakeHit(int rawAttack) { int dmg = Mathf.Max(1, rawAttack - stats.defense); currentHP = Mathf.Max(0, currentHP - dmg); return dmg; } }

Inventory with item assets

Items are ScriptableObjects too. The inventory is a plain C# list of stacks. Because items are assets, a potion's icon, name, and effect all travel together.

Item.cs & Inventory.cs
using System.Collections.Generic; using UnityEngine; [CreateAssetMenu(menuName = "RPG/Item")] public class Item : ScriptableObject { public string itemName; public Sprite icon; public int healAmount; // 0 for non-consumables } public class Inventory : MonoBehaviour { [System.Serializable] public class Stack { public Item item; public int qty; } public List<Stack> stacks = new(); public System.Action OnChanged; public void Add(Item item, int amount = 1) { var s = stacks.Find(x => x.item == item); if (s != null) s.qty += amount; else stacks.Add(new Stack { item = item, qty = amount }); OnChanged?.Invoke(); } public bool Use(Item item, Combatant on) { var s = stacks.Find(x => x.item == item); if (s == null || s.qty <= 0) return false; if (item.healAmount > 0) on.currentHP = Mathf.Min(on.stats.maxHealth, on.currentHP + item.healAmount); if (--s.qty == 0) stacks.Remove(s); OnChanged?.Invoke(); return true; } }
Tip.
Combine this with the save system from lesson 2.14: serialize the inventory as a list of item name + quantity, and on load look the assets back up by name. Never serialize the ScriptableObject reference itself to JSON.

Branching dialogue

Dialogue is a tiny graph: a node has text and a set of choices, each pointing to the next node. Model nodes as ScriptableObjects and you have an authorable conversation tree.

DialogueNode.cs
using UnityEngine; [CreateAssetMenu(menuName = "RPG/DialogueNode")] public class DialogueNode : ScriptableObject { [TextArea] public string line; public Choice[] choices; [System.Serializable] public class Choice { public string text; public DialogueNode next; // null = end conversation } }
DialogueRunner.cs
using UnityEngine; public class DialogueRunner : MonoBehaviour { DialogueNode current; public System.Action<DialogueNode> OnNode; public void Begin(DialogueNode root) { current = root; OnNode?.Invoke(current); } public void Choose(int index) { if (current == null || index >= current.choices.Length) return; current = current.choices[index].next; OnNode?.Invoke(current); // null node = your UI closes the panel } }

Turn-based combat

Turn order is decided by speed; each turn the active combatant acts and we advance. A coroutine keeps the flow readable.

BattleManager.cs
using System.Collections; using System.Collections.Generic; using System.Linq; using UnityEngine; public class BattleManager : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] Combatant hero; [SerializeField] Combatant enemy; IEnumerator Start() { var order = new List<Combatant> { hero, enemy } .OrderByDescending(c => c.stats.speed).ToList(); while (hero.IsAlive && enemy.IsAlive) { foreach (var actor in order) { if (!actor.IsAlive) continue; Combatant target = actor == hero ? enemy : hero; int dealt = target.TakeHit(actor.stats.attack); Debug.Log($"{actor.stats.displayName} hits for {dealt}"); yield return new WaitForSeconds(0.6f); if (!target.IsAlive) break; } } Debug.Log(hero.IsAlive ? "Victory!" : "Defeat..."); } }
Lab.
Replace the hero's auto-attack with a player menu: pause the turn and present Attack / Item / Flee buttons (uGUI, lesson 2.13). Item opens the inventory and calls Use; Attack resumes the coroutine. This converts the demo into a real playable battle.
Watch out.
ScriptableObject fields are shared assets. If combat modified StatBlock directly, every Goblin would share one HP pool. Always store mutable state (currentHP) on the runtime Combatant, never on the SO.
🎮 Build This: RPG — Embergate

This starts a new genre: the RPG. You can set it in the top-down adventure world you began in lesson 2.13, reusing its HUD and save system.

  1. Create StatBlock assets for the hero and a few enemies, and Item assets including a healing potion.
  2. Give the player an Inventory and an item-grid UI bound to OnChanged.
  3. Author a branching conversation with DialogueNode assets and show it through DialogueRunner when the player talks to an NPC.
  4. Trigger a battle scene with BattleManager for hero vs enemy turn order by speed.
  5. Implement the Attack/Item/Flee menu Lab so combat is player-driven.
  6. Hook the save system so inventory and current HP persist between sessions.

title: Puzzle Games: Physics Joints, Match-3 Grids & Tweening
html:
Two flavors of puzzle

Puzzle games split into two camps that teach different muscles. Physics puzzles (Angry Birds, Cut the Rope, bridge builders) are about emergent behavior from joints and springs. Match-3 (Bejeweled, Candy Crush) is about grid logic — matching, clearing, gravity, and cascades — finished with smooth tweening. We will build a slice of each.

Physics joints and springs

Unity's joints constrain how two Rigidbodies move relative to each other. The 2D family includes HingeJoint2D (a pivot, like a pendulum or door), SpringJoint2D (a stretchy connection), and DistanceJoint2D (a rigid rod). A swinging rope is just a chain of segments connected by hinge joints; a slingshot is a spring joint that yanks a projectile toward an anchor.

Slingshot.cs
using UnityEngine; [RequireComponent(typeof(SpringJoint2D), typeof(Rigidbody2D))] public class Slingshot : MonoBehaviour { SpringJoint2D spring; Rigidbody2D rb; bool launched; Camera cam; void Awake() { spring = GetComponent<SpringJoint2D>(); rb = GetComponent<Rigidbody2D>(); cam = Camera.main; rb.isKinematic = true; // frozen while we aim } void OnMouseDrag() { if (launched) return; Vector2 m = cam.ScreenToWorldPoint(Input.mousePosition); transform.position = m; // pull back against the spring anchor } void OnMouseUp() { if (launched) return; rb.isKinematic = false; // release: spring snaps it forward launched = true; Invoke(nameof(Detach), 0.2f); } void Detach() => spring.enabled = false; // fly free once launched }
Tip.
Set the spring's Connected Anchor to the slingshot's fork. Tune Frequency (springiness) and Damping Ratio (bounce) to make the launch feel snappy rather than wobbly.

The match-3 grid

A match-3 board is a 2D array of gem types. The two core algorithms are match detection (find runs of 3+ same-colored gems horizontally or vertically) and collapse (drop gems into cleared gaps, refill from the top). Represent gems with an enum or color id.

Match3.cs (detection)
using System.Collections.Generic; using UnityEngine; public class Match3 : MonoBehaviour { public int W = 8, H = 8; int[,] grid; // gem color id per cell, -1 = empty public List<Vector2Int> FindMatches() { var matched = new HashSet<Vector2Int>(); // horizontal runs for (int y = 0; y < H; y++) for (int x = 0; x < W - 2; x++) { int c = grid[x, y]; if (c >= 0 && c == grid[x+1, y] && c == grid[x+2, y]) { matched.Add(new(x, y)); matched.Add(new(x+1, y)); matched.Add(new(x+2, y)); } } // vertical runs for (int x = 0; x < W; x++) for (int y = 0; y < H - 2; y++) { int c = grid[x, y]; if (c >= 0 && c == grid[x, y+1] && c == grid[x, y+2]) { matched.Add(new(x, y)); matched.Add(new(x, y+1)); matched.Add(new(x, y+2)); } } return new List<Vector2Int>(matched); } }

Collapse and cascades

After clearing matches to -1, gems above fall to fill gaps, then empty cells at the top get new random gems. A cascade happens when the refilled board produces new matches — loop detect/clear/collapse until FindMatches returns empty.

Match3.cs (collapse)
public void Collapse() { for (int x = 0; x < W; x++) { int write = 0; // lowest empty row to fill for (int y = 0; y < H; y++) if (grid[x, y] != -1) { grid[x, write] = grid[x, y]; if (write != y) grid[x, y] = -1; write++; } for (int y = write; y < H; y++) grid[x, y] = Random.Range(0, 5); // refill top with 5 colors } }
Law.
Keep gameplay logic (the int[,] grid) completely separate from visuals (the gem GameObjects). The grid is the source of truth; the visuals merely animate toward what the grid already decided. This makes match-3 testable and bug-free.

Tweening for polish

Snapping gems to new cells instantly looks awful. Tweening interpolates a value over time with an easing curve. You can use a coroutine or a library like DOTween, but the core idea is simple.

Tween.cs
using System.Collections; using UnityEngine; public static class Tween { public static IEnumerator Move(Transform t, Vector3 to, float dur) { Vector3 from = t.position; for (float e = 0; e < dur; e += Time.deltaTime) { float k = e / dur; k = 1f - (1f - k) * (1f - k); // ease-out quad t.position = Vector3.LerpUnclamped(from, to, k); yield return null; } t.position = to; } }
Lab.
Wire swap input: on click-drag between two adjacent cells, swap them in the grid, run FindMatches; if there are matches, clear/collapse/cascade and award score; if not, tween the gems back to their original cells (the classic "illegal move" bounce-back).
🎮 Build This: Physics puzzle + Match-3 — Gizmo Drop & Gem Cascade

This starts a new genre: puzzle games, built as two small scenes so you practice both styles.

  1. Physics scene: build a slingshot with a SpringJoint2D projectile and a stack of physics blocks to knock over; add a rope from chained HingeJoint2D segments.
  2. Match-3 scene: generate an 8x8 grid of colored gem prefabs from the int[,] board.
  3. Implement FindMatches and Collapse; loop them for cascades until stable.
  4. Add the swap-input Lab with legal/illegal-move handling.
  5. Use the Tween coroutine so every gem fall, swap, and bounce-back animates smoothly.
  6. Add a score counter that rewards cascade chains with a multiplier.

title: Endless Runner & Roguelike: Procedural Generation & Difficulty
html:
Why procedural generation

Both genres in this lesson share one engine: procedural generation (procgen) — building levels from code instead of hand-placing every object. An endless runner streams an infinite track of chunks ahead of the player; a roguelike stitches together a fresh dungeon of rooms each run, with permadeath making every choice matter. We will build the generation core for each and a difficulty curve that ramps over time.

Endless runner: chunk streaming

The trick to "endless" is an illusion: the world is a handful of chunk prefabs spawned ahead of the player and recycled behind them (object pooling from lesson 2.15 keeps it GC-free). The player can even stay still while the world scrolls toward them — but spawning ahead is the cleaner pattern.

ChunkSpawner.cs
using System.Collections.Generic; using UnityEngine; public class ChunkSpawner : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] GameObject[] chunkPrefabs; [SerializeField] Transform player; [SerializeField] float chunkLength = 20f; [SerializeField] int chunksAhead = 5; readonly Queue<GameObject> active = new(); float spawnZ = 0f; void Start() { for (int i = 0; i < chunksAhead; i++) Spawn(); } void Update() { // when player passes a chunk, recycle the oldest and add a new one if (player.position.z - chunkLength > spawnZ - chunksAhead * chunkLength) { Destroy(active.Dequeue()); Spawn(); } } void Spawn() { var prefab = chunkPrefabs[Random.Range(0, chunkPrefabs.Length)]; var go = Instantiate(prefab, new Vector3(0, 0, spawnZ), Quaternion.identity); active.Enqueue(go); spawnZ += chunkLength; } }
Tip.
Give each chunk prefab a few obstacle spawn points and randomize which are populated. Weighted randomness — common gaps, rarer hard jumps — produces variety without unfair spikes.

Difficulty over time

An endless runner that never speeds up gets boring; one that ramps too fast gets brutal. Drive difficulty from a single curve so it is tunable in the Inspector.

DifficultyDirector.cs
using UnityEngine; public class DifficultyDirector : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] AnimationCurve speedOverTime; // authored in Inspector [SerializeField] float maxTime = 120f; public float CurrentSpeed { get; private set; } float t; void Update() { t += Time.deltaTime; float k = Mathf.Clamp01(t / maxTime); CurrentSpeed = speedOverTime.Evaluate(k); } }

Roguelike: procedural rooms

A roguelike dungeon is a graph of rooms connected by doors. A simple, robust generator is the random walk / drunkard's walk on a grid, or placing rooms and connecting them with corridors. Here is a compact room-grid generator that guarantees connectivity by always linking each new room to the previous one.

DungeonGenerator.cs
using System.Collections.Generic; using UnityEngine; public class DungeonGenerator : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] GameObject roomPrefab; [SerializeField] GameObject corridorPrefab; [SerializeField] int roomCount = 10; [SerializeField] float spacing = 12f; public void Generate(int seed) { Random.InitState(seed); // same seed = same dungeon (great for sharing/debug) var occupied = new HashSet<Vector2Int>(); Vector2Int cell = Vector2Int.zero; occupied.Add(cell); PlaceRoom(cell); Vector2Int[] dirs = { Vector2Int.up, Vector2Int.down, Vector2Int.left, Vector2Int.right }; for (int i = 1; i < roomCount; i++) { Vector2Int next; int guard = 0; do { next = cell + dirs[Random.Range(0, 4)]; } while (occupied.Contains(next) && ++guard < 20); PlaceCorridor(cell, next); cell = next; occupied.Add(cell); PlaceRoom(cell); } } void PlaceRoom(Vector2Int c) => Instantiate(roomPrefab, new Vector3(c.x * spacing, 0, c.y * spacing), Quaternion.identity, transform); void PlaceCorridor(Vector2Int a, Vector2Int b) { Vector3 mid = new Vector3((a.x + b.x) * 0.5f * spacing, 0, (a.y + b.y) * 0.5f * spacing); Instantiate(corridorPrefab, mid, Quaternion.identity, transform); } }
Law.
Always generate from a seed via Random.InitState. A seeded run is reproducible — you can replay a bug, share a daily challenge, or write tests. "Random" without a seed is undebuggable.

Permadeath and the run loop

Permadeath means death wipes the run and you start fresh — but meta-progression (unlocks that persist) keeps it rewarding. Tie this to the save system from lesson 2.14: clear run state on death, but keep the unlock data.

RunManager.cs
using UnityEngine; public class RunManager : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] DungeonGenerator dungeon; public int Depth { get; private set; } void Start() => NewRun(); public void NewRun() { Depth = 0; dungeon.Generate(System.Environment.TickCount); // fresh seed each run } public void OnPlayerDeath() { Debug.Log($"You died at depth {Depth}. Run over."); // persistent unlocks saved here; run state discarded NewRun(); } public void Descend() { Depth++; dungeon.Generate(System.Environment.TickCount + Depth); } }
Lab.
Add a difficulty hook to the dungeon: scale roomCount and enemy spawn density by Depth so each floor is harder. For the runner, feed DifficultyDirector.CurrentSpeed into both the player's forward speed and the obstacle-density weighting.
🎮 Build This: Endless runner + Roguelike — Forever Forward & Deep Descent

This starts a new genre: procedural games, built as two scenes that share the procgen mindset.

  1. Runner scene: auto-run a 3D character forward; stream chunks with ChunkSpawner and recycle them behind the player.
  2. Add lane-switch and jump input, plus randomized obstacles per chunk.
  3. Add DifficultyDirector with an authored speed curve and ramp the run over two minutes.
  4. Roguelike scene: generate a connected dungeon with DungeonGenerator from a seed; print the seed so runs are reproducible.
  5. Add RunManager with permadeath: death restarts a fresh run, descending generates a harder floor.
  6. Apply the difficulty Lab to both games and persist meta-unlocks via the save system.

title: Fighting & Rhythm Games: Frame Data, Combos & Beat Timing
html:
Games measured in frames and milliseconds

Fighting and rhythm games are the most timing-precise genres in this track. A fighting game lives or dies by frame data: how many frames a move takes to start up, stay active, and recover, plus hitboxes/hurtboxes and input buffering for combos. A rhythm game is built on beat timing: judging the player's input against the music's tempo with tight accuracy windows. Both demand a frame- and time-aware mindset.

Hitboxes and hurtboxes

A hurtbox is the region where a character can be hit; a hitbox is the region of an attack that deals damage. They live on different layers and overlap detection (a trigger collider) decides a hit. The hitbox is only enabled during a move's active frames.

Hitbox.cs
using UnityEngine; public class Hitbox : MonoBehaviour { public int damage = 5; public Fighter owner; void OnTriggerEnter2D(Collider2D other) { var hurt = other.GetComponent<Hurtbox>(); if (hurt != null && hurt.owner != owner) // don't hit yourself hurt.owner.TakeHit(damage, owner); } }

Frame data as a move definition

Define moves as data: startup, active, and recovery measured in frames (at 60 FPS, one frame is ~16.7 ms). A coroutine plays the move, toggling the hitbox only during active frames.

Move.cs & Fighter.cs
using System.Collections; using UnityEngine; [CreateAssetMenu(menuName = "Fight/Move")] public class Move : ScriptableObject { public string moveName; public int startup = 6, active = 3, recovery = 12; // in frames public int damage = 5; } public class Fighter : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] GameObject hitbox; public int health = 100; public bool Busy { get; private set; } const float FRAME = 1f / 60f; public void Perform(Move m) { if (!Busy) StartCoroutine(Run(m)); } IEnumerator Run(Move m) { Busy = true; yield return Frames(m.startup); // wind-up: vulnerable hitbox.SetActive(true); hitbox.GetComponent<Hitbox>().damage = m.damage; yield return Frames(m.active); // active: can connect hitbox.SetActive(false); yield return Frames(m.recovery); // recovery: punishable Busy = false; } IEnumerator Frames(int n) { for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) yield return new WaitForSeconds(FRAME); } public void TakeHit(int dmg, Fighter from) { health -= dmg; Debug.Log($"{name} hit for {dmg}, hp {health}"); } }
Law.
Frame advantage is the heart of fighting games: if your move recovers before the opponent's, you are "plus on block" and can act first. Authoring startup/active/recovery as data lets you balance the whole roster without touching code.

Input buffering for combos

Players rarely hit the next input on the exact frame a move ends. An input buffer remembers a button press for a short window so a queued attack fires the instant the character is free — this is what makes combos feel responsive instead of dropping inputs.

InputBuffer.cs
using UnityEngine; public class InputBuffer : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] Fighter fighter; [SerializeField] Move punch; [SerializeField] float bufferWindow = 0.15f; // ~9 frames float bufferedUntil = -1f; void Update() { if (Input.GetButtonDown("Fire1")) bufferedUntil = Time.time + bufferWindow; // remember the press if (Time.time <= bufferedUntil && !fighter.Busy) { fighter.Perform(punch); bufferedUntil = -1f; // consumed } } }

Rhythm: beat timing windows

Rhythm games judge a press against the nearest beat. The key is to drive timing from AudioSettings.dspTime (the audio hardware clock), which is far more accurate than Time.time. Compute each beat's timestamp from the BPM, then grade the player's hit by how close they were.

BeatJudge.cs
using UnityEngine; public class BeatJudge : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] float bpm = 120f; double startDsp; double SecondsPerBeat => 60.0 / bpm; void Start() => startDsp = AudioSettings.dspTime; void Update() { if (Input.GetButtonDown("Fire1")) Judge(); } void Judge() { double songPos = AudioSettings.dspTime - startDsp; double beat = songPos / SecondsPerBeat; double error = System.Math.Abs(beat - System.Math.Round(beat)) * SecondsPerBeat; string grade = error < 0.04 ? "PERFECT" : error < 0.09 ? "GREAT" : error < 0.15 ? "GOOD" : "MISS"; Debug.Log($"{grade} (off by {error*1000:0}ms)"); } }
Tip.
Never schedule audio or judge beats with Time.time — frame-rate hitches will desync the music. AudioSettings.dspTime is sample-accurate, and you can schedule clips precisely with audioSource.PlayScheduled(dspTime).
Lab.
For the rhythm game, spawn note objects that travel toward a hit line, each timed so it arrives exactly on its beat (use dspTime to position them). Grade the press against the nearest active note instead of a pure metronome, and feed PERFECT/GREAT/GOOD into a combo multiplier.
🎮 Build This: Fighting + Rhythm — Frame Trap & Beat Breaker

This starts a new genre: fighting and rhythm, built as two focused scenes.

  1. Fighting scene: two Fighter characters with hurtboxes and a child hitbox object; author Move assets with startup/active/recovery frame data.
  2. Drive attacks through the InputBuffer so queued punches chain into combos.
  3. Add health bars (uGUI, lesson 2.13) and a round/KO state.
  4. Rhythm scene: play a looping track and grade presses with BeatJudge from dspTime.
  5. Implement the note-spawning Lab so notes scroll to a hit line on the beat.
  6. Show a PERFECT/GREAT/GOOD popup and a combo counter that resets on MISS.

title: Audio Mixer, Particle System, Shader Graph & Juicing It
html:
The difference between a prototype and a game

Every game you have built so far is mechanically complete — but a great game feels alive. This lesson is about juice: the audio, particles, shaders, and micro-feedback that make actions satisfying. None of it changes the rules; all of it changes how the game feels. We will layer four polish systems across the games you have already built.

The Audio Mixer

Playing clips with AudioSource.Play works, but you need control: master volume, separate music/SFX sliders, ducking music under important sounds, and effects like reverb. The Audio Mixer (Window > Audio > Audio Mixer) gives you routing groups. Route every SFX source to an SFX group and music to a Music group, both under Master. Expose a group's volume parameter to script for a settings menu.

VolumeSettings.cs
using UnityEngine; using UnityEngine.Audio; public class VolumeSettings : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] AudioMixer mixer; // 0..1 from a UI slider -> decibels (logarithmic) public void SetSFXVolume(float v) { float dB = Mathf.Log10(Mathf.Clamp(v, 0.0001f, 1f)) * 20f; mixer.SetFloat("SFXVolume", dB); // exposed parameter name } }
Tip.
Volume is logarithmic. A linear 0-1 slider mapped straight to dB sounds wrong — use Mathf.Log10(v) * 20 so the slider feels natural. Expose the parameter by right-clicking the volume in the mixer and choosing Expose to script.

The Particle System

Unity's built-in ParticleSystem (the Shuriken system) is your workhorse for explosions, muzzle flashes, dust, and pickups. Its modules — Emission, Shape, Color over Lifetime, Size over Lifetime — are configured in the Inspector, but you trigger and tune them from code.

ImpactFX.cs
using UnityEngine; public class ImpactFX : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] ParticleSystem hitBurst; public void PlayAt(Vector3 pos, Vector3 normal) { hitBurst.transform.position = pos; hitBurst.transform.rotation = Quaternion.LookRotation(normal); hitBurst.Emit(20); // fire a one-shot burst of 20 particles } }
Tip.
For a one-shot effect, set the system to not Play On Awake, set Stop Action to Destroy, and instantiate it where you need it — or call Emit() on a pooled system to avoid instantiation cost (object pooling, lesson 2.15).

Shader Graph

Shader Graph is Unity's node-based shader editor (URP/HDRP). You build effects visually — no HLSL required — like a dissolve, a hit flash, a force-field fresnel glow, or scrolling water. The classic combat polish is a hit flash: when a character takes damage, flash them white for a few frames. Expose a _FlashAmount property in the graph (lerp the albedo toward white by that amount), then drive it from code.

HitFlash.cs
using System.Collections; using UnityEngine; public class HitFlash : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] Renderer rend; static readonly int Flash = Shader.PropertyToID("_FlashAmount"); MaterialPropertyBlock mpb; void Awake() => mpb = new MaterialPropertyBlock(); public void Flick() => StartCoroutine(Do()); IEnumerator Do() { for (float t = 0; t < 0.12f; t += Time.deltaTime) { rend.GetPropertyBlock(mpb); mpb.SetFloat(Flash, 1f - t / 0.12f); // fade flash out rend.SetPropertyBlock(mpb); yield return null; } rend.GetPropertyBlock(mpb); mpb.SetFloat(Flash, 0f); rend.SetPropertyBlock(mpb); } }
Tip.
Use a MaterialPropertyBlock instead of renderer.material. Touching .material clones the material (a leak and a draw-call killer); property blocks set per-instance values for free.

Game feel: shake, hit-stop, tween

Three cheap techniques carry enormous weight. Screen shake (Cinemachine Impulse, lesson 2.10) on impacts. Hit-stop — freezing time for a few frames on a heavy hit — makes blows land with weight. Tweens (lesson 2.21) on UI and pickups (a quick scale-pop) add life everywhere.

HitStop.cs
using System.Collections; using UnityEngine; public class HitStop : MonoBehaviour { public static HitStop Instance; void Awake() => Instance = this; public void Freeze(float seconds) => StartCoroutine(Do(seconds)); IEnumerator Do(float seconds) { Time.timeScale = 0f; // WaitForSecondsRealtime ignores the frozen timeScale yield return new WaitForSecondsRealtime(seconds); Time.timeScale = 1f; } }
Watch out.
While Time.timeScale = 0, anything using Time.deltaTime stops too. Use WaitForSecondsRealtime and Time.unscaledDeltaTime for code that must keep running during hit-stop, or you will freeze forever.
Lab.
Build a reusable Juice helper that, on any hit, fires all four at once: Emit a particle burst, trigger Cinemachine impulse shake, call HitStop.Freeze(0.05f), and Flick() the hit flash. Drop it into your FPS, third-person shooter, and fighting game and feel the difference one method makes.
🎮 Build This: Juice pass — across all your games

This is a concept lesson; rather than a new genre, you extend the games you already built (the FPS from 2.16, the third-person shooter from 2.17, the fighting game from 2.23) with a polish pass.

  1. Create an Audio Mixer with Master/Music/SFX groups; route all sources and wire a settings slider via VolumeSettings.
  2. Add a particle ImpactFX burst at every hitscan/raycast impact point.
  3. Build a Shader Graph hit-flash material with a _FlashAmount property and drive it with HitFlash on enemies.
  4. Add HitStop and Cinemachine impulse shake on heavy hits and kills.
  5. Combine them into the Juice helper from the Lab and call it from your damage code.
  6. A/B test: toggle the juice off and on and notice how mechanically identical gameplay feels completely different.
